problem_summary: 2022 MCM Problem B (discrete). The Colorado River basin (Lake Powell/Glen Canyon Dam upstream, Lake Mead/Hoover Dam downstream) is drying out. Teams must build a model that (1) determines how to draw general-usage water and hydropower from the two dams to meet fixed demand across five states (AZ, CA, WY, NM, CO), how long supply lasts at given water levels M and P, and how much extra water is needed; (2) sets a rule for resolving competition between general-usage water and hydropower; (3) handles the shortage case where supply < demand; (4) responds to changing affected-area population/agriculture/industry, increased renewable share, and conservation measures; and (5) communicate results as an article for the journal Drought and Thirst. This paper answers all five with a three-model stack: a network LP for allocation, a marginal-utility valuation layer, and a first-price auction (IPV) for scarcity.
models_used: Model 1 — Resources Allocation Network (RAN): a directed flow network coupling the two dams in series (Glen Canyon power-release water flows into Lake Mead), with conservation constraints on general water and hydropower, lake-volume update recursions, a cylinder-volume approximation to convert stored volume V_i to head h_i, and a hydraulic power equation w_ij = beta_i * rho_water * u_ij * g * h_i from conservation of mechanical energy.

Model 1 formulated as a time-varying linear program: minimize total water drawn (sum of general-usage v_ij + hydropower-release u_ij) subject to per-state water/electricity demand equalities, volume-update equations, head/volume relation, the power equation, and a head-dependent generation cap (no generation when h_i < h_i^lowest).

Time-Varying Linear Optimization Algorithm (Algorithm 1): an iterative per-time-step LP solve with branching on whether each lake still has water/head, advancing volumes and heads until both dams drop below minimum levels; records t1 (first dam stops generating) and t2 (system fails).

Model 2 — Marginal Utility valuation model: defines an I_i (Resource_i) marginal-utility efficiency function in [0,1] that peaks when water:hydropower hit a target ratio, an isoelastic Utility_i function (CRRA-style with state-specific elasticities mu_i^water, mu_i^elec), and a composite Value_i = I_i * Utility_i giving each state a private valuation of a resource bundle; explicitly framed around Pareto optimality.

Model 3 — Independent Private Value (IPV) first-price sealed-bid auction: N=5 states are risk-neutral bidders with private valuations drawn from distributions H_i on common support, a reserve price R, equilibrium bid functions phi_i and inverse bids lambda_i, derived via the bidder's expected-surplus maximization and first-order conditions. A configurable fraction of the scarce inflow (505 m^3/s) is auctioned; the remainder is split equally.

Generalized Backwards-Shooting (BS) Algorithm (Algorithm 2, after Gayle & Richard 2008 / Marshall et al. 1994): numerically solves the asymmetric first-price IPV auction by Taylor-series expansions of H_i^{-1} on an equally spaced valuation grid (Algorithm 3 builds the inverse-CDF grid via a root finder, B-spline interpolation using IMSL DBSNAK/DBSINT, and BSCPP piecewise-polynomial conversion).

A penalty (fine) rule: Fine_total(i) = Fine_basic(i) + Fine_extra(i) = i*Fine_basic + Utility(i), tying the marginal extra fine to the utility/opportunity-cost of wasted resources, used to fund the auction/investment loop.
key_techniques: Series-coupled reservoir modeling: the downstream lake's inflow is set equal to the upstream dam's power-release outflow (V_2 update uses sum of u_1j), capturing the physical cascade rather than treating dams independently.

Reducing a hydraulic-physics problem to an LP by linearizing storage updates and embedding the energy-conversion equation as a constraint, then solving step-by-step (time-varying LP) so head-dependent generation limits stay valid each period.

Explicit head-threshold logic: water can still be supplied below the power-generation minimum head but above the dead-pool minimum, encoded as a piecewise generation cap — a realistic and judge-pleasing distinction.

Importing microeconomics (diminishing marginal utility + isoelastic utility) to convert the qualitative 'competing interests' requirement into a quantitative, per-state Value function — turning Problem 2's vague 'standard for competition' into math.

Auction theory as the scarcity-allocation mechanism (Problem 3): instead of a heuristic priority rule, the team uses a first-price IPV auction with reserve price so that allocation tends toward Pareto efficiency and generates funds redistributed to losing states for renewable-energy build-out — a self-described closed-loop, positive-feedback design.

Reusing a published numerical auction solver (Gayle & Richard generalized backwards-shooting with B-spline/IMSL machinery) rather than inventing one, lending credibility and tractability to an otherwise hard equilibrium computation.

Scenario/sensitivity coupling: perturbation parameter sigma applied to population/agriculture/industry and to renewable share, reporting that 'days the model lasts' changes by <=3 days to argue robustness; auction fraction (50/70/100%) swept to show inter-state equity improves and total value rises.

Strong narrative scaffolding: a master flowchart, notation table, and the article ('Attention Please: Time to Save') restating RAN, marginal utility, and the IPV closed loop for a lay journal audience.
why_it_won: "It reframed a logistics/allocation prompt as a three-layer economics-of-scarcity story and executed each layer with named, citable methods. Judges reward novelty grounded in real theory: bringing marginal utility and first-price IPV auction theory (with a published numerical solver) into a water-allocation problem is genuinely distinctive versus the typical LP-only or greedy-priority submissions. The structure maps cleanly onto the problem's sub-parts — RAN/LP answers 'how long and how much extra water,' the Value function answers 'standard for competition,' and the auction answers 'what to do under shortage' — so a judge can check off every requirement. It also closes the loop the problem implicitly wants: auction revenue funds renewable build-out for the states that lose, addressing sustainability and equity simultaneously, plus a concrete fine rule for the conservation sub-question. The physics is respectable (head-dependent power, series cascade, dead-pool vs power-pool thresholds), the visualization is heavy and varied (Sankey, double pie, network diagrams, sensitivity curves), and the writing carries a clear 'save the river' thesis. The combination of mechanistic rigor + economic novelty + full requirement coverage + polished communication is the Outstanding profile."
section_highlights: "Summary cleanly previews all three models and their roles, and states concrete numbers (21-day horizon, scheme is 'more economical and sustainable'). The master flowchart (Figure 2) and notation table front-load the architecture so the reader never gets lost across three interlocking models. Section 4 builds the LP from first principles equation-by-equation (conservation -> volume update -> cylinder head -> power equation -> generation cap -> objective), then gives explicit pseudo-code (Algorithm 1) with the case-branching on lake state — this transparency is a hallmark. Results are reported in three water-level regimes (average/min-power/max) with day-by-day allocation numbers per state, and rendered as pie, Sankey, and line charts. Section 6 motivates the auction with a real-world waste-and-fine narrative and a closed-loop framework diagram before stating the IPV equilibrium FOC and citing the exact numerical solver (Gayle & Richard). The sensitivity section reframes the problem's Part-4 'what if' questions directly as perturbation experiments. The standalone magazine article at the end nails the communication deliverable in accessible language."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The cylinder approximation for lake volume->head (Assumption 5) is crude; the team admits it introduces error but waves it off — real bathymetry is highly non-linear and head drives power output, so this propagates into the LP.

The auction layer is theoretically heavy but thinly validated: the IPV results table (Table 3) shows the 'none for auction' and '50% for auction' rows are numerically identical, suggesting the auction's effect was not actually computed or was mislabeled — a credibility crack if a judge probes it. Table 3's caption is also mis-copied ('List of Water Level Constraints').

Private-value distributions H_i and elasticities mu_i^water/mu_i^elec are asserted/calibrated 'based on demand' without data justification; the whole valuation/auction edifice rests on these unestimated parameters.

The mechanism mixes incommensurate things — fines fund bids, bids fund renewable investment — without budget accounting or a check that auction proceeds actually suffice for desalination/solar build-out; the closed loop is narrative, not quantified.

Units and figures are inconsistent/garbled in places (Sankey tables show placeholder '100' values and repeated rows; pie-chart category lists are scrambled), indicating rushed figure generation that survived only because the prose carried the argument.
Robustness claim ('error <= 3 days') is asserted from a small sigma sweep on a single output metric (days lasted); it does not test sensitivity of the auction allocations themselves.

Model 1's marginal-utility / generation-physics realism is stronger than the rigor of how the three models are actually chained numerically — the hand-off from Value_i into the auction's H_i is described conceptually but not shown end-to-end.
reusable_patterns: Three-tier model stack for any allocation-under-scarcity prompt: (1) a network/LP that handles the 'enough resources' base case, (2) a valuation layer that quantifies competing stakeholders, (3) a market/auction mechanism for the genuine-shortage case. This cleanly partitions a multi-part problem.

Convert a vague 'fairness / competition rule' requirement into a scalar Value function by composing a normalized efficiency term (marginal utility in [0,1], peaking at a target mix) with an isoelastic CRRA utility — a transferable recipe for turning qualitative stakeholder language into optimizable numbers.

Use a first-price IPV auction with a reserve price as the allocation rule when the problem says 'who gets it when there isn't enough,' and pair it with a revenue-redistribution loop so 'losers' are compensated — this directly serves equity + sustainability sub-questions graders look for.

Borrow a published numerical solver (here: generalized backwards-shooting with B-spline/Taylor-series inverse-CDF) and cite it, rather than inventing an equilibrium solver under time pressure — buys rigor cheaply.
Series-coupling physical subsystems by feeding one unit's output as the next unit's input constraint (downstream lake inflow = upstream power release) — generalizes to any cascade/pipeline modeling.
Encode regime thresholds as piecewise constraints (supply allowed below power-pool but above dead-pool head) to add realism and give richer, more defensible results.
Answer Part-4 'what-if' sub-questions by literally running them as sensitivity perturbations (sigma sweeps over demand drivers and renewable share) and reporting change in a headline output metric.
Always ship the communication deliverable as a self-contained, jargon-light article that restates each model's role with its own figures — treat it as a first-class section, not an afterthought.
